rationale_for_baseline_tier: An intensely itchy but systemically well patient with a benign non-contagious autoimmune rash needs prompt (within a few days) in-person confirmation and symptom relief — a T2 baseline. It is low acuity; the only escalation is for secondary skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Scratched areas becoming hot, spreading red, draining pus, or fever
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates secondary bacterial infection needing antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the scratched skin becomes hot, spreads red, or oozes yellow pus, or you get a fever, see a doctor the same day for possible antibiotics.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly reassure no isolation is required to reduce social/psychological distress.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is a non-contagious autoimmune rash — no need to isolate. Use the steroid cream and a night antihistamine to control the itch and sleep, keep your nails short, and see your GP within a week to confirm with a skin biopsy and coeliac blood tests. See a doctor sooner if the scratched skin becomes hot, spreading red, or oozes pus.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: GP within 1 week; same-day if infection signs
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: No after-hours care needed unless secondary infection develops.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth impression of dermatitis herpetiformis (symmetric extensor grouped vesicles, no burrows/contacts); reassured non-contagious; topical steroid + night antihistamine advised; biopsy/immunofluorescence and coeliac serology recommended.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing without in-person confirmation or a secondary-infection safety-net.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Secondary impetiginisation/cellulitis of excoriated skin is missed and worsens.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/urgent escalation or household scabies isolation for a benign non-contagious rash is over-triage.
